findings.claim: Automattic Inc. was founded on June 20, 2005, by Matt Mullenweg to support and monetize the open-source WordPress project he co-founded in 2003

Automattic is headquartered in San Francisco, California, but operates as a fully distributed company with employees in nearly 100 countries. The company closed its physical SF office in 2017 due to low usage.
Toni Schneider served as CEO from 2006 until 2014, when Matt Mullenweg took over the role and remains CEO as of 2026
Automattic's stated mission is to 'democratize publishing and commerce'
Series A (Jan 2006): $1.1M from Polaris Partners, Radar Partners, CNET, True Ventures
Series B (Jan 2008): $29.5M from Polaris Partners, Radar Partners, The New York Times Company, True Ventures
Secondary market transaction (2013): Tiger Global invested $75M to provide liquidity to early shareholders
Series C (May 2014): $160M from Insight Partners, Iconiq Capital, Tiger Global, Chris Sacca
Series D (Sep 2019): $300M solely from Salesforce Ventures, valuing the company at $3 billion
Series E (Feb 2021): $288M from BlackRock, Wellington Management, Alta Park, ICONIQ
In August 2021, Automattic conducted a $250M share buyback at a $7.5 billion peak valuation ($85/share)
Total funding raised across 12 rounds (including secondary market): approximately $986 million. No new primary funding rounds (Series F) in 2024 or 2025.
CEO Matt Mullenweg reported in late 2024 the company generates approximately $500M in annual revenue; external estimates placed 2024 revenue between $700M-$710M
BlackRock issued repeated markdowns: Oct 2024 to ~$43.85/share (51% drop), Mar 2025 to $31.03/share, Jun 2025 to $27.74/share (cumulative 67.4% decline from 2021 purchase price)
Implied valuation as of early 2026 is estimated between $2.4B and $2.5B, down from $7.5B peak in 2021
Automattic is described as operating at 'break-even or better' as of mid-2025, prioritizing leaner operations through workforce reductions
Automattic has acquired at least 27 companies since 2005

Key acquisitions: Gravatar (Oct 2007), PollDaddy/Crowdsignal (Oct 2008), Simplenote/Simperium (Jan 2013), Longreads (Apr 2014), WooCommerce/WooThemes (May 2015, ~$30M), Tumblr (Aug 2019, from Verizon), Parse.ly (Feb 2021), Day One (Jun 2021), Pocket Casts (Jul 2021), Texts (Oct 2023, ~$50M), ActivityPub (Mar 2023), Beeper (Apr 2024, ~$125M), Harper (Nov 2024), WPAI (Dec 2024), Clay (Jun 2025)
WooCommerce (acquired 2015) powers over 20% of all e-commerce sites globally
WordPress (maintained by Automattic) powers more than 43% of all websites on the internet as of 2025

Primary revenue streams: WordPress.com hosting subscriptions (Starter/Explorer/Creator/Entrepreneur tiers), WooCommerce extensions and WooPayments transaction fees, WordPress VIP enterprise hosting (clients include Meta, CNN, Salesforce), Jetpack/Akismet security SaaS, Tumblr ads and subscriptions, Day One and Pocket Casts premium subscriptions
Emerging revenue: AI data licensing deals with OpenAI and Midjourney for anonymized Tumblr/WordPress.com public data (early 2024), WordAds advertising network
In October 2024, 159 employees (8.4% of staff) departed via 'voluntary alignment' buyout packages offered during the WP Engine dispute
In April 2025, Automattic laid off approximately 280 employees (16% of workforce)
The WP Engine conflict began at WordCamp US in September 2024 when Mullenweg called WP Engine a 'cancer to WordPress' and blocked their access to WordPress.org resources
In December 2024, a federal judge granted WP Engine a preliminary injunction ordering Automattic to restore WordPress.org access
In February 2025, a class-action lawsuit was filed against Automattic by WP Engine customers alleging economic harm from service disruptions

As of March 2026, the WP Engine vs Automattic lawsuit remains active and unresolved with no settlement reached; WP Engine filed a Third Amended Complaint in Feb 2026 adding WooCommerce and WordPress Foundation as defendants

Automattic is the largest corporate contributor to WordPress open source, historically employing over 100 full-time staff for core contributions and pledging nearly 2,000 hours/week in 2024 via the Five for the Future initiative

key_insights: Automattic is a ~$2.5B (down from $7.5B peak) privately-held distributed company with ~$500-700M annual revenue, ~27 acquisitions, and ~986M total funding across 5 primary rounds -- but its valuation has been marked down 67% by BlackRock since 2021 amid the WP Engine controversy and broader market correction

The business model is a multi-pronged freemium ecosystem: WordPress.com hosting subscriptions, WooCommerce transaction fees and extensions, WordPress VIP enterprise contracts, Jetpack/Akismet SaaS, plus diversified bets on Tumblr, messaging (Beeper/Texts), podcasting (Pocket Casts), and AI data licensing

The WP Engine legal dispute (Sep 2024-present) has been a defining crisis -- triggering a preliminary injunction, class-action lawsuit, 8.4% voluntary departures, 16% layoffs in April 2025, and significant investor confidence erosion that explains much of the valuation decline
gaps: Exact audited revenue figures -- Automattic is private and does not disclose financials; the $500M (CEO statement) vs $700M (external estimate) gap is unresolved
Tumblr acquisition price -- widely rumored at $3M but no official confirmation from Automattic vs Yahoo's original $1.1B purchase price
Current headcount as of March 2026 after the April 2025 layoffs -- estimates suggest ~1,400-1,500 but no official number published
Detailed revenue breakdown by product line (WordPress.com vs WooCommerce vs VIP vs Jetpack) -- not publicly disclosed
Terms of the Salesforce Ventures Series D relationship post-2024 given Salesforce's own strategic shifts
